basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Takāthur — “Vying for Increase” — diagnoses a sickness of the soul in every age: the race for accumulation and pride. But the sūra rises gradually, and ends by naming three degrees of knowing — what would become, among the Sufis, the three yaqīn. Tustarī explains them with a rare image: “Certainty (yaqīn) is the fire; affirmation by the tongue is the wick; and the deed is its oil. Certainty begins with unveiling (mukāshafa, مُكَاشَفَة), then comes direct contemplation (muʿāyana, مُعَايَنَة), then witnessing (mushāhada, مُشَاهَدَة).” The soul distracted by possessions becomes the opposite of a lamp; and the sūra recalls it, in few words, to the lamp it truly is. And the end — “then you will be questioned on that Day about the blessings” — is not a threat: it is, says Qushayrī, the reminder that God has given you so many things that call for gratitude, from cool water on a summer day to the Prophet himself.

versets.commentaire: alhā (أَلْهَىٰ) — to divert, to distract. The same root gives lahw, amusement: what occupies without nourishing. Qushayrī: “Your rivalry in having has busied you.” The soul is made for something else; when it absorbs itself in counting what it has, it loses what it is. The sūra does not condemn possession — it observes that it distracts. Possession becomes an idol when it takes the attention.

mots.def: alhā: to divert, to distract; perfect + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: takāthur: reciprocal form of the root k-th-r; the race to amass, rivalry by number.

versets.commentaire: Qushayrī adds the detail: tradition reports that the Quraysh boasted by counting their living, and then went to the cemetery to count their dead in order to enlarge their tally. And the sūra turns the word: you have visited the graves — not as tourists, but as future residents. The race of having stretches even into the bone: one still counts on the day one enters.

kallā — the rupture. And Tustarī, on this verse, has God say a word of strange tenderness: “The one who turned away from Me shall know that he will not find another like Me.” Then he cites this verse: You will remember Me if you have tasted any other than Me, / And you will know then that I was for you a treasure. The promised knowing is not a threat but a return: the soul will come back to itself when all its alternatives have been exhausted.

versets.trad: Again, nay! Soon you shall know!
versets.commentaire: The repetition is not in vain. It says that there are two moments of knowing — one at death, one at the Resurrection; or: one for the deniers, one for the believers. Or more simply: what was not grasped the first time will be grasped the second. Grace does not tire of insisting.

versets.trad: Nay! If you knew with the knowledge of certainty —
versets.commentaire: ʿilm al-yaqīn (عِلْم اليَقِين), the knowledge of certainty — the first of the three degrees of yaqīn. Tustarī gives the image that lights the whole sūra: “Certainty is the fire, affirmation by the tongue is the wick, the deed is the oil. Certainty begins with unveiling (mukāshafa), then comes direct contemplation (muʿāyana), then witnessing (mushāhada).” The soul is a lamp: it needs a wick (the word) and an oil (the act) for certainty to catch fire. When it catches, it begins to light the place.

mots.def: yaqīn: certainty; in Sufism, a state of knowing without doubt.

versets.commentaire: al-jaḥīm (الجَحِيم), the blaze — a name of the Fire. The verse extends the unfinished sentence of the previous one: if you knew by the knowledge of certainty, you would see. Seeing is not knowing, and it is not imagination either — it is a whole degree of knowing.

mots.gram: energetic imperfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: al-Jaḥīm: name of the Fire, the raging blaze; from the root j-ḥ-m.

versets.trad: then you would surely see it with the eye of certainty;
versets.commentaire: ʿayn al-yaqīn (عَيْن اليَقِين), the eye of certainty. Tustarī specifies: “The eye of certainty is not an aspect of certainty — it is the thing itself, in its entirety (kulliyya).” Knowledge had named; the eye sees. This is what the Sufi tradition will then name, in a third degree, ḥaqq al-yaqīn — the truth of certainty, where one is no longer only a witness but is within the thing itself.

mots.def: raʾā: to see; energetic imperfect + feminine suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: energetic imperfect + suffix pronoun, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: ʿayn: the eye; by extension, direct vision, actual perception.

versets.trad: then you will surely be questioned, on that Day, about ease and delight.
versets.commentaire: al-naʿīm (النَّعِيم). Qushayrī unfolds: “He has meant all the blessings God has given them, and He demands of them the gratitude for these.” And he lists, with grave humour: “among the naʿīm about which one is questioned: the lightening of prescriptions and the facilities in worship; or warm water in winter and cool water in summer; or health of the body and free time; or contentment with destiny (al-riḍāʾ bi-l-qaḍāʾ); or satisfaction in daily life; or the Prophet himself, the Chosen One (al-Muṣṭafā).” Tustarī moves in the same direction: “Not an hour of day or night passes by jinn and men without containing a right (ḥaqq) that God holds over them.” The verse does not threaten — it recalls: every breath is a gift, and gratitude is the only payment God awaits for having given them.

mots.def: saʾala: to ask, to question; passive energetic imperfect plural.
mots.gram: passive energetic imperfect, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: yawm + idh: on that day, on that very day.
naʿīm: ease, blessing; according to Qushayrī, all that God gives — from the humblest daily thing to the Prophet himself.
